meta.name: Caribbean Jerk Chicken
meta.subtitle: Jamaican-style jerk chicken with a smoky-spicy allspice + thyme profile (grill/oven)

source.author: SSA Canonical Catalog
source.attribution: Modeled from common Jamaican jerk technique: aromatic spice paste + thyme/scallion + heat, cooked over high heat with optional smoke.

yield.notes: Assumes bone-in chicken pieces. Scale marinade linearly.

ingredients.label: Chicken pieces (bone-in thighs/drumsticks or quarters)
ingredients.notes: Skin-on recommended for best char. You can slash thicker pieces to help seasoning penetrate.

ingredients.label: Lime juice
ingredients.notes: For brightness and to help the jerk paste cling.

ingredients.label: Ginger (rough chopped)
ingredients.notes: Fresh is preferred; 1 tbsp ground can substitute.

ingredients.label: Allspice (ground) or crushed berries
ingredients.notes: Allspice is the core jerk note.

ingredients.label: Ground cinnamon
ingredients.notes: Optional warm note (some households include it).

ingredients.label: Apple cider vinegar (or white vinegar)
ingredients.notes: Adds tang and helps preserve the paste for a day or two.

ingredients.label: Scotch bonnet (or habanero)
ingredients.notes: 2 peppers is hot. For milder: use 1 or remove seeds.

ingredients.label: Salt
ingredients.notes: Adjust based on soy sauce saltiness.

ingredients.label: Neutral oil
ingredients.notes: Helps paste spread and promotes browning.

ingredients.label: Liquid smoke
ingredients.notes: Optional if you are baking instead of grilling/smoking.

ingredients.label: Pimento wood chips (or hickory/fruitwood chips)
ingredients.notes: Traditional jerk uses pimento wood. Optional for grill smoke box.

steps.title: Make the jerk seasoning paste
instructions: Add scallions, onion, garlic, ginger, thyme leaves (or dried thyme), allspice, black pepper, brown sugar, soy/tamari, lime juice, salt, oil, and scotch bonnet(s) to a blender/food processor.
Blend into a thick paste. Add 1–2 tbsp water only if needed to get it moving.
Optional: add vinegar and/or a tiny splash of liquid smoke if oven-baking and you want smokier flavor.
Taste carefully (tiny dab). Adjust salt, sugar, or heat to preference.

steps.title: Coat and marinate the chicken
instructions: Pat chicken dry. Optional: make 1–2 shallow slashes in thick areas to help the jerk penetrate.
Rub jerk paste all over chicken (under the skin where possible).
Cover and marinate at least 2 hours, ideally overnight (8–24 hours).

steps.title: Cook (grill preferred, oven supported)
instructions: GRILL (best): Preheat grill to medium-high (two-zone if possible). Oil grates. If using wood chips, set up smoke box/foil packet.
Start chicken skin-side down over indirect heat, cover, and cook ~25–35 minutes, turning occasionally to avoid burning.
Move to direct heat to char and crisp skin, 2–4 minutes per side as needed.
OVEN (easy): Heat oven to 425°F. Place chicken on a rack over a baking sheet. Roast 35–50 minutes, flipping once halfway. Broil 1–3 minutes to finish char if desired.
Cook until thickest piece reaches 165°F internal (thighs can go 175–185°F for best texture). Rest 10 minutes.

steps.title: Serve
instructions: Serve jerk chicken hot with rice and peas, festival bread, or fried plantains.
Add a squeeze of lime if desired.
Optional: serve with a cooling side (slaw) to balance the heat.

adapt.notes: Marinate 30–60 minutes if needed. Grill or roast 18–25 minutes total. Aim for 175°F for best texture.
Use 1 scotch bonnet, remove seeds/ribs, or substitute 1/2 habanero + 1 sweet pepper. Increase brown sugar slightly.
Finely mince aromatics and combine with spices/soy/lime/oil into a rough paste. Massage into chicken and marinate longer (overnight recommended).
Roast at 425°F on rack. Broil briefly for char. Add liquid smoke only if desired; real broil char usually suffices.

fridge.notes: Cool quickly; store airtight. Reheat until steaming hot.

freezer.notes: Freeze cooked chicken portions. Thaw overnight and reheat in oven/air fryer for best texture.

reheat.notes: Oven/air fryer restores skin best. Microwave is fastest but softens skin.

cookOnceEatTwice: Cook extra for wraps, bowls, or salads the next day
